Policy Name/Title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 – Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act)
Summary: Horizontal EU law establishing risk-based obligations, bans for certain practices, requirements for high‑risk AI (e.g., quality management, data governance, human oversight), transparency duties for specific systems, and market surveillance/enforcement mechanisms. Entered into force on 1 Aug 2024 with staggered application timelines.
Healthcare Implications: Many clinical AI products will fall under existing MDR/IVDR as medical devices and also under the AI Act as high‑risk AI (Annex II/III cross‑references), driving additional documentation, post‑market monitoring, and transparency. Hospitals and vendors should align conformity assessment, data governance, and human‑oversight controls across MDR/IVDR and the AI Act.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: MDCG 2019-11 – Guidance on Qualification and Classification of Software under MDR/IVDR
Summary: Updated MDCG guidance on when software, including AI/ML functions, qualifies as a medical device and how to classify it under MDR/IVDR. Clarifies decision steps, borderline cases, and alignment with other MDCG docs; adds examples and notes on updates/versions.
Healthcare Implications: Essential reference for determining if clinical AI is SaMD and for assigning class, which governs evidence and oversight. Product and regulatory teams should map features against the decision tree and classification rules to scope clinical evidence and PMS/PMCF.

Policy Name/Title: Reflection paper – Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the Medicinal Product Lifecycle
Summary: EMA reflection paper setting expectations for use of AI/ML across the medicines lifecycle–R&D, clinical trials, manufacturing, and post‑authorisation. Addresses governance, data quality, validation, GxP alignment, and human oversight; provides considerations for submissions and inspections.
Healthcare Implications: Sponsors using AI in trial design, data analysis, pharmacovigilance, or manufacturing should plan transparent methods, validation packages, and risk management. Clinicians participating in trials may see AI‑enabled tools subject to additional documentation and controls.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law (CETS No. 225)
Status: Open for signature; not yet in force
Summary: First legally binding international treaty on AI addressing human rights, democracy, and rule of law. Sets baseline obligations for parties to ensure AI lifecycle activities respect fundamental rights and provide effective safeguards and remedies.
Healthcare Implications: Health authorities and providers in signatory states should expect cross‑cutting obligations around rights impact assessment, transparency, and accountability in clinical AI. National implementing laws and guidance may flow from the Convention and apply to healthcare delivery and research.

Policy Name/Title: OECD Recommendation on Artificial Intelligence (OECD/LEGAL/0449)
Summary: First intergovernmental standard on AI adopted by the OECD Council. Sets principles for trustworthy, human‑centred AI and policy recommendations for national strategies, research, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a governance benchmark referenced by many health‑sector policies. Health ministries and systems can map internal AI programs to the Recommendation’s principles to support accountability and cross‑border alignment.

Policy Name/Title: Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Global ethical standard adopted by 193 Member States covering human rights, inclusion, data governance, education, and environmental sustainability in AI. Encourages national implementation via action plans and monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Health systems can use the Recommendation to shape equitable AI policies, informed consent and transparency practices, and workforce training. Reinforces safeguards for vulnerable populations in clinical AI deployment.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias

Policy Name/Title: Ethics and Governance of Artificial Intelligence for Health – WHO Guidance
Summary: WHO guidance outlining ethical challenges and six consensus principles for AI in health, with recommendations for governance, regulation, and rights‑preserving design.
Healthcare Implications: Useful baseline for hospital AI committees and ministries to establish ethics reviews, bias assessment, and transparency practices for AI used in care and public health.

Policy Name/Title: Regulatory Considerations on Artificial Intelligence for Health
Summary: Nonbinding resource describing regulatory considerations for AI in health across the product lifecycle. Addresses data quality, risk management, transparency, post‑market monitoring, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Manufacturers and regulators can use this as a checklist when designing oversight for AI‑enabled health technologies. Hospitals should anticipate documentation needs for procurement and clinical governance.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Ethics and Governance of AI for Health – Guidance on Large Multi‑Modal Models (LMMs)
Summary: WHO guidance focusing on LMMs in health use cases, covering safety, effectiveness, privacy, bias, accountability, and workforce impacts. Recommends governance measures, evaluation, and monitoring tailored to generative AI systems.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals piloting GenAI (e.g., documentation assistants, patient‑facing chat) should align with LMM‑specific safeguards, including transparent labeling, guardrails, and post‑deployment monitoring.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Privacy & Data; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias

Policy Name/Title: Software and AI as a Medical Device (SaMD/AIaMD) Change Programme – Roadmap
Issuing Body: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency
Summary: MHRA’s multi‑work‑package programme to update regulation of software and AI as medical devices across the lifecycle, including qualification, pre‑market evidence, post‑market monitoring, and transparency.
Healthcare Implications: Signals evolving UK expectations for AI medical devices. Developers and NHS providers should track deliverables that affect submissions, labeling, and post‑market surveillance.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Impact of AI on the Regulation of Medical Products – Policy Paper
Summary: Sets out MHRA’s approach to medicines/devices regulation in the age of AI, including evidence expectations, adaptivity, international recognition, and collaboration with IMDRF and other regulators.
Healthcare Implications: Informs sponsors and NHS partners about the regulator’s priorities, including transparency and post‑market monitoring for AI‑enabled products. Useful for aligning development plans and regulatory strategies.

Policy Name/Title: Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (v1.0)
Summary: Singapore’s model governance framework tailored to Generative AI, adding dimensions like content provenance, model evaluation/testing, incident reporting, and accountability while building on the 2019/2020 framework. Includes suggested practices and implementation levers.
Healthcare Implications: Useful for hospitals and vendors deploying GenAI for documentation, triage, and patient engagement. Provides concrete controls (e.g., testing, red‑teaming, transparency) that complement clinical safety and privacy requirements.

Policy Name/Title: Guiding Principles for Conducting Clinical Trial for Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices (MLMD)
Summary: Joint HSA–MFDS principles for designing and conducting clinical trials of ML‑enabled medical devices. Covers study design, dataset considerations, performance endpoints, drift/updates during trials, and transparency of algorithms.
Healthcare Implications: Provides practical expectations for sponsors and investigators running MLMD trials in Asia. Hospitals participating in such trials can align protocols, monitoring, and change‑management to the guidance.

Policy Name/Title: Guidance on Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices (MLMD)
Summary: Final guidance defining MLMD scope and expectations for evidence, change management (including PCCPs), and lifecycle oversight in pre‑market submissions. Aligns terminology to IMDRF N67 and encourages robust performance evaluation and real‑world monitoring.
Healthcare Implications: Canadian sponsors and partners should structure submissions and validation to meet ML‑specific requirements; healthcare providers adopting MLMD can expect clearer labeling and post‑market commitments.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Artificial intelligence (AI) explained – Medical devices
Summary: TGA overview of how AI and software are regulated as medical devices, including when AI is considered a device, evidence expectations, and current initiatives on GenAI/LMMs.
Healthcare Implications: Sets expectations for sponsors regarding classification, evidence, and compliance in Australia. Hospitals can use this to understand which AI tools fall under TGA regulation and what assurances to expect.

Policy Name/Title: Regulation of software, including AI – SaMD rules and guidance
Summary: Detailed guidance on how the TGA regulates software-based medical devices, including SaMD definitions, classification, evidence, and obligations across the lifecycle. Recently updated to reflect AI considerations.
Healthcare Implications: Helps developers and providers determine when AI features trigger device regulation and what documentation is required. Supports procurement due diligence and clinical governance for AI-enabled software.

Policy Name/Title: Interim Measures for the Management of Generative AI Services
Issuing Body: Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) and others
Summary: CAC‑led measures regulating public‑facing generative AI services, emphasizing content governance, security assessments, data quality, and user rights. Requires providers to take responsibility for generated content and to implement risk controls.
Healthcare Implications: Impacts China‑based health apps and GenAI tools used by patients/clinicians, imposing content and data controls. International vendors operating in China must adapt service configurations and compliance processes.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy

Policy Name/Title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
Summary: Government charter committing agencies to transparency, accountability, and oversight in the use of algorithms, including risk assessment and public communication.
Healthcare Implications: Public health agencies in NZ using AI/algorithms should meet Charter commitments–useful reference for transparency and engagement with Māori data sovereignty considerations.

Policy Name/Title: Regulatory Considerations on Artificial Intelligence for Health (FG‑AI4H DEL02)
Summary: ITU/WHO Focus Group deliverable summarizing regulatory considerations for AI in health, including ethics, risk management, performance evaluation, and international cooperation.
Healthcare Implications: Useful overview for ministries and regulators designing AI oversight and for providers seeking to understand cross‑jurisdictional expectations.

Policy Name/Title: Good Machine Learning Practice for Medical Device Development – Guiding Principles (IMDRF/AIML WG/N88 FINAL:2025)
Summary: International guiding principles for Good Machine Learning Practice across the medical device lifecycle, developed by IMDRF’s AIML Working Group.
Healthcare Implications: Device developers and hospital innovators can align development, validation, and change management of AI‑enabled devices with these principles; supports harmonized evidence expectations and post‑market practices.

Policy Name/Title: Machine Learning‑enabled Medical Devices – Key Terms and Definitions (AIMD WG)
Summary: Defines core terms for ML‑enabled medical devices to promote consistent regulatory language and scope across jurisdictions.
Healthcare Implications: Enables consistent classification, documentation, and evaluation of MLMD across regulators and clinical users; often referenced by national guidances (e.g., Health Canada MLMD).
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems
